weather.tip: 86% rain chance and overcast skies most of the day — carry an umbrella and budget extra time for waterlogging-prone underpasses and ORR stretches.

around.headline: Two child deaths at Bengaluru schools this week put safety practices under scrutiny
around.summary: A 4-year-old was crushed under her sister's school van near the Dasanapura underpass as her mother watched; separately, a Class 6 boy died during a morning drill and the school's PT teacher was booked for murder after parents alleged assault. Worth asking your child's school directly about van reversing/attendant protocols and drill supervision.

around.headline: 7 killed in lorry-MUV collision; 2 teen workers killed separately on Tumakuru-Bengaluru highway
around.summary: Two unrelated fatal highway collisions were reported in the last two days on routes many commuters use for weekend/work travel out of the city. A reminder to avoid overtaking heavy vehicles on undivided highway stretches, especially in low light or rain.

around.headline: Greater Bengaluru Authority starts clearing abandoned vehicles citywide from tomorrow, July 10
around.summary: GBA's five city corporations begin a crackdown from July 10 on vehicles abandoned on roads and footpaths. If you have a car or bike parked long-term on a public street, expect it to be towed — move it before tomorrow.
around.source: The New Indian Express / The Times of India

around.headline: Footpath vendors clash with Safe Footpath Campaign; some asked to pay ₹5,000 to fight eviction
around.summary: Malleswaram vendors say they're being asked to pay ₹5,000 toward legal costs to resist footpath clearance, and a citywide vendor protest/bandh has been called against the campaign. Pedestrians may see clearer footpaths in some areas even as the dispute continues.

around.headline: CM floats Bengaluru South, possibly Harohalli, for the city's second airport
around.summary: Chief Minister D.K. Shivakumar indicated the second airport will likely come up in south Bengaluru, with Harohalli (in his own constituency) among sites being considered, pending a feasibility report. Land near any confirmed site could see fast-moving real estate and infrastructure interest over the next few years.

around.headline: Fresh US strikes on Iran keep oil elevated; Indian markets partly recover
around.summary: Trump ordered fresh strikes on Iran and Brent crude hit a two-week high on Strait of Hormuz worries, even as the Sensex rose about 550 points on value buying. If the standoff drags on, keep an eye on petrol/diesel prices when planning monthly fuel or travel budgets.

events.name: Bengaluru International Short Film Festival (BISFF)
events.note: This week's pre-show gala already wrapped; the main festival with citywide screenings runs mid-August.

events.name: Chess Festival at Bhartiya Mall of Bengaluru
events.note: Featuring AGM Praveen Sagar; open to walk-ins.

events.name: Sankara Eye Hospitals & VISION 2020 India eye-care conference
events.when: Announced this week, exact dates awaited
events.note: Public eye-care conference in Bengaluru; watch for the schedule.

street_smart.title: A customer just paid you with a fake ₹500 note. Now what?
street_smart.based_on: Real case reported today: a Bengaluru MBA graduate, previously jailed for currency counterfeiting, restarted a fake-note printing operation — police traced him after he used one of his own fake notes to buy cigarettes.
street_smart.situation: You run a small shop. A regular-looking customer buys something and pays with a ₹500 note. After they leave, you notice it feels unusually smooth, slightly see-through in the wrong places, and the green security thread doesn't shift colour when you tilt it. It would be easy to just mix it back into your till with the rest of the day's cash. What do you do?

choices.text: Keep it in the till and spend it in your own purchases tomorrow — it's a small loss either way, and reporting it just costs you time.
choices.feedback: This is exactly how fake notes stay in circulation — every shopkeeper who thinks 'not my problem' passes the loss to the next person, until someone gets caught (like the counterfeiter this week, traced through a single accepted note). Knowingly passing on a fake note is itself an offense — you can turn from victim into accused.

choices.text: Confront the customer loudly on the spot and accuse them of counterfeiting.
choices.feedback: Understandable reaction, but most people who pass a fake note don't know it's fake — they were probably given it somewhere else. Publicly accusing someone risks an unnecessary confrontation over ₹500. Calmly ask them to wait while you double-check, rather than escalating.

choices.text: Check the note against a genuine one for the see-through registration mark, colour-shifting ink on the '500', and the windowed security thread reading 'भारत' and 'RBI'. If it fails, set it aside — don't spend it — and report/deposit it instead.
choices.feedback: Correct. Genuine RBI notes have several checkable security features; if two or more fail, treat it as fake. Banks will impound a counterfeit note without paying you for it — that's a real loss, but the alternative is knowingly putting fake currency back into circulation, which is a bigger problem and a legal risk.

choices.text: Assume it's fine — it's one note among hundreds, and cigarette customers wouldn't bother faking money — and put it straight into the till.
choices.feedback: Small, everyday transactions are exactly where counterfeit ₹500 notes are designed to blend in unnoticed. Skipping the check on 'ordinary' purchases is how fake notes circulate for months before anyone catches them.

takeaways: Genuine ₹500 notes carry a see-through registration mark, colour-shifting ink on the numeral, a windowed security thread with 'भारत'/'RBI', and microtext — check at least two of these before accepting large notes from strangers.
Knowingly passing on a fake note you received (even unintentionally) is itself a criminal offense — isolate it and report it, don't spend it.
Banks are required to impound counterfeit currency without compensating you — expect to lose the face value, but don't compound the loss by becoming complicit.

quiz.q: From which date is the Greater Bengaluru Authority running a citywide drive to clear abandoned vehicles from roads and footpaths?
quiz.why: The drive, covering all five city corporations under GBA, starts July 10 — GBA replaced the BBMP as Bengaluru's top civic authority.

quiz.q: CM D.K. Shivakumar indicated Bengaluru's second airport will most likely come up in:
options: South Bengaluru, with Harohalli among the sites
quiz.why: Harohalli, which falls within the CM's own constituency, is among the south Bengaluru sites being weighed pending a feasibility report.

quiz.q: This week's fresh US strikes on Iran and fears over the Strait of Hormuz pushed Brent crude oil to:
quiz.why: Even as oil hit a two-week high, Indian markets partly shrugged it off — the Sensex rose about 550 points on value buying.

quiz.q: In RBI monetary policy, the 'repo rate' refers to:
options: The rate at which RBI lends short-term funds to banks
The tax rate on repatriated foreign income
The rate of return on repossessed property
quiz.why: Changes in the repo rate ripple into home-loan and EMI interest rates within weeks, since banks price their lending off it.

quiz.q: On a genuine Indian ₹500 note, the windowed security thread is inscribed with which repeating text?
quiz.why: The thread also shifts colour from green to blue when tilted — one of several checks worth knowing given this week's Bengaluru fake-currency bust.
